elements.sent-2411-001: We investigate the interplay between quantum theory and gravity by exploring gravitational lensing and Einstein ring images in a weak gravitational field induced by a mass source in spatial quantum superposition.
elements.sent-2411-002: We analyze a quantum massless scalar field propagating in two distinct models of gravity: the first quantized Newtonian gravity (QG) model, which generates quantum entanglement between the mass source and other systems, and the \schrodinger-Newton (SN) gravity model, which does not produce entanglement.
elements.sent-2411-003: Visualizing the two-point correlation function of the scalar field, we find that the QG model produces a composition of multiple Einstein rings, reflecting the spatial superposition of the mass source.
elements.sent-2411-004: By contrast, the SN model yields a single deformed ring image, representing a classical spacetime configuration.
elements.sent-2411-005: Furthermore, we introduce a specific quantity named the which-path information indicator and visualize its image.
elements.sent-2411-006: The QG model again reveals multiple Einstein rings, while the image intensity in the SN model notably vanishes.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our findings provide a visual approach to witness gravity-induced entanglement through distinct features in Einstein ring images.
elements.sent-2411-008: This study advances our understanding of quantum effects in general relativistic contexts and establishes a foundation for future studies of other relativistic phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-009: Building a quantum gravity theory remains one of the foremost challenges in theoretical physics.
elements.sent-2411-010: One of the main challenges is the lack of experimental evidence to test the quantum aspects of gravity.
elements.sent-2411-011: Colella et al. conducted the pioneering experiment that bridged quantum physics and gravity~\cite{Colella1975 , which measured the phase shift in the quantum state of neutrons within the gravitational field of the Earth.}
elements.sent-2411-012: This foundational work has gained significant attention for investigating quantum phenomena influenced by gravity and for inspiring several related experiments over the years~\cite{Asenbaum2017,Overstreet2022,Schlippert2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Despite examining the quantum characteristics of the probe system in a classical gravitational field, they offer only limited insight into the quantum nature of gravity itself.
elements.sent-2411-014: To test the quantum nature of gravity, Feynmann proposed a thought experiment to examine how a gravitational field behaves when its source is placed in a quantum superposition~\cite{Feynmann .}
elements.sent-2411-015: This idea inspired the experimental proposal by Bose, Marletto, Vederal, et al., which aimed to verify whether the gravitational interaction induced by spatially superposed massive objects can generate quantum entanglement between them~\cite{Bose2017,Marletto2017 , collectively referred to as the BMV papers.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Their idea was based on a fundamental concept from quantum information theory: local operations and classical communication (LOCC) cannot create quantum entanglement between two systems.
elements.sent-2411-017: In their proposal, two massive objects obey quantum mechanics and interact solely through Newtonian gravity.
elements.sent-2411-018: If the initially separable state of the two masses evolves into an entangled state over time, this would indicate that their mutual gravitational interaction is no longer LOCC.
elements.sent-2411-019: Let us consider a setup in which a quantum test particle with mass $m$ and position operator $\hat\x$ is influenced by a gravitational mass source with mass $M$ and position operator $\hat \X$ to illustrate the idea behind the BMV papers.
elements.sent-2411-020: The evolution of the test particle is governed by the Newtonian gravitational potential created by the mass source.
elements.sent-2411-021: Although a fully verified quantum-compatible theory of gravity remains unestablished, let us introduce some models of Newtonian gravity in the context of quantum mechanics as specific examples.
elements.sent-2411-022: As a first model, we suppose that the gravitational interaction can be represented by the following first-quantized form of the classical Newtonian potential \begin{equation}\label{eq:intro_QG} m\Phi(\hat\x,\hat\X)=\frac{-GmM}{|\hat\x-\hat \X|}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-023: In this study, we refer to this first-quantized Newtonian gravity as the quantized gravity (QG) model.
elements.sent-2411-024: This interaction depends on the test particle position operator and the mass source position operator, suggesting that the gravitational potential inherits the quantum fluctuation of the mass source position.
elements.sent-2411-025: Moreover, the interaction couples the operators of the test particle and the mass source, resulting in the quantum entanglement between the two systems.
elements.sent-2411-026: As a counterpart to the QG model, let us also consider alternative gravity models, often referred to as semi-classical gravity theories, which predict the absence of such an entanglement~\cite{Bahrami2014,Anastopoulos2014,Ruffini1969,Kibble1978,Kibble1980,Diosi1989,Diosi2011,Penrose1996,Penrose2014,Kafri2014,Tilloy2016,Bassi2017,Carney2023,Oppenheim2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: In these models, gravity remains fundamentally classical, although the gravitational sources themselves are treated quantum mechanically.
elements.sent-2411-028: A notable example is the \schrodinger-Newton (SN) gravity model, one of the simplest semi-classical models.
elements.sent-2411-029: In this framework, the gravitational interaction experienced by the test particle from the mass source object is given by the following: \begin{equation}\label{eq:intro_SN} m\Phi(\hat\x)=\left\langle \frac{-GmM}{|\hat\x-\hat \X|} \right\rangle_mass source.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-030: Taking the expectation value over the mass source system in the SN gravity model eliminates the direct dependence on the mass source position operator.
elements.sent-2411-031: As such, the gravitational potential does not inherit the quantum fluctuation of the mass source position.
elements.sent-2411-032: In addition, the operators between the two systems are not directly coupled; thus, no entanglement can arise in this gravity model.
elements.sent-2411-033: Although the SN model has some theoretical problems, such as enabling superluminal signals and inconsistencies with the Newtonian limit of the semi-classical Einstein equation~\cite{Bahrami2014,Anastopoulos2014 , it still serves as a simple counterpoint to the QG model, specifically in terms of whether gravitational interaction can induce entanglement.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Therefore, the experimental framework proposed in the BMV papers provides a way to test whether gravitational interaction operates as LOCC and thus distinguishes between various models, including QG and SN.
elements.sent-2411-035: The experimental proposal of the BMV papers is now considered feasible in the near future owing to recent advances in quantum technology, potentially providing the first robust evidence for the quantum nature of gravity.
elements.sent-2411-036: Indeed, techniques have been developed to maintain quantum coherence even in mesoscopic-scale objects~\cite{Panda2024,Bild2023,Fein2019 , and precise measurements of gravitational interactions in microscopic systems have also been achieved~\cite{Westphal2021,Lee2020 .}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Various ideas have also been proposed to improve the feasibility of detecting gravity-induced entanglement by optimizing the experimental setup~\cite{Balushi2018,Miao2020,Matsumura2020,Miki2024,Krisnanda2020,Kaku2023,Fujita2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Further advances in experimental techniques and ingeniously designed setups are also expected to enable table-top tests of gravitational behavior in a quantum system with masses approaching the Planck scale.
elements.sent-2411-039: Notably, although the BMV proposal presents a significant guideline for experimental proposals of the quantum aspects of Newtonian gravity, it does not provide sufficient evidence of the quantization of the gravitational field or the existence of gravitons.
elements.sent-2411-040: This is because the experiment is designed to test Newtonian gravity, which does not involve the dynamical degrees of freedom of the gravitational field.
elements.sent-2411-041: Some interesting studies have explored the relationship between gravity-induced entanglement and the existence of gravitons~\cite{Carney2022,Martin2023,Belenchia2018,Hidaka2022,Sugiyama2023 ; however, the primary goal of the BMV papers is to determine whether non-dynamical Newtonian gravity can be classified as LOCC or not.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Building on the thought experiment by Feynman and the BMV papers, recent efforts have focused on relativistic extensions to explore the quantum nature of gravity beyond the Newtonian regime~\cite{Zych2018,Chen2023,Giacomini2019,Kaku2022,Foo2023relativity,Foo2021,Foo2022,Foo2023minkowski,Kabel2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-043: Specifically, these studies investigate the behavior of non-dynamical curved spacetime induced by a gravitational mass source in quantum superposition, aiming to understand the quantum properties of gravity in the relativistic regime.
elements.sent-2411-044: Although probing quantum phenomena of relativistic gravity is more challenging than in the Newtonian case, this research direction is motivated by the theoretical interest in uncovering quantum features unique to gravity and bridging the idea of the BMV proposals toward the ultimate goal of constructing the quantum gravity theory.
elements.sent-2411-045: In addition, an experimental motivation exists: in the study of relativistic regimes, we expect to see differences between the quantum effects of gravity and those of other quantum interactions, such as electromagnetic interaction.
elements.sent-2411-046: Such distinctions could help rule out potential loopholes in gravity-induced entanglement experiments, thereby ensuring that observed quantum signals are indeed due to gravity.
elements.sent-2411-047: Various arguments regarding the quantum nature of relativistic gravity have been established based on these motivations.
elements.sent-2411-048: These include studies on quantum mechanical phenomena~\cite{Kaku2022,Foo2023relativity and quantum field theoretical phenomena~\cite{Foo2021,Foo2022,Foo2023minkowski,Kabel2022 in non-dynamical curved spacetimes induced by a superposed mass source.}}
elements.sent-2411-049: As an extension into further relativistic regimes, some studies have investigated the quantum phenomena of the dynamical gravitational field in the linear regime~\cite{Kanno2021,Mehdi2023,Biswas2023 , and the non-linear regime, corresponding to quantum gravity theory.}
elements.sent-2411-050: This study explores the quantum effect of general relativistic phenomena beyond the Newtonian regime, specifically examining the gravitational lensing of quantum fields in non-dynamical curved spacetimes induced by the superposed mass source treated within the framework of quantum mechanics.
elements.sent-2411-051: Gravitational lensing, a well-known phenomenon in general relativity, refers to the light scattering caused by the gravitational field~\cite{Carroll2019,Schneider1999 .}
elements.sent-2411-052: When light emitted from a light source passes near a massive object, it is attracted by the gravitational force of the object, causing the light to scatter behind the mass following the geodesics along the curvature of spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-053: An observer situated on the opposite side of the light source across the massive object will perceive an image of the light altered by this gravitational lensing.
elements.sent-2411-054: Specifically, when the light source, the massive object, and the observer are aligned in a line, the observer sees a ring-shaped image of the light, known as the Einstein ring, which has been confirmed in several observations~\cite{Hewitt1988,EHT2019,EHT2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-055: In addition, the scattering angle in gravitational lensing differs by a factor of two compared to the predictions from Newtonian gravity, highlighting its unique nature as a relativistic effect.
elements.sent-2411-056: Gravitational lensing has also been extensively studied in the context of wave optics and classical massless field theory~\cite{Nakamura1999,Schneider1999,Nambu2013,Kanai2013,Nambu2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-057: Moreover, several studies have explored the gravitational lensing of quantum fields, particularly in relation to Hawking radiation scattering in classical curved spacetime~\cite{Nambu2022,Caribe2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-058: A recent study~\cite{Lee2024 has further examined the gravitational lensing of quantum fields in the context of specific quantum gravity theories proposed by Verlinde and Zurek~\cite{Verlinde2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-059: Furthermore, Biswas et al.~\cite{Biswas2023 studied tree-level photon-matter scattering via graviton exchange.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Although our focus is not on gravitons, our study adopts a similar approach to that of Biswas et al. in exploring the gravitational coupling between light and matter.
elements.sent-2411-061: This paper investigates the Einstein ring image in a curved spacetime induced by spatially superposed mass sources.
elements.sent-2411-062: For practical calculations, we assume the background spacetime is a weak gravitational field, with the Newtonian potential incorporated into the metric being described by either the QG model in Eq.~\eqref{eq:intro_QG or the SN gravity model in Eq.~\eqref{eq:intro_SN .}}
elements.sent-2411-063: We calculate the evolution of the quantum massless scalar field as a toy model to study light propagation in this background spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-064: In addition, we assume that the observer interacts with the system via the Unruh-DeWitt (UDW) detectors~\cite{Unruh1976 that are coupled to the scalar field.}
elements.sent-2411-065: We introduce two kinds of observables accessible through the UDW detectors: the two-point correlation function of the scalar fields, which is commonly examined in the context of quantum field theory and wave optical formalism of gravitational lensing, and a newly defined quantity named the which-path information indicator, useful for witnessing gravity-induced entanglement.
elements.sent-2411-066: Then, we construct the Einstein ring image from these two observables.
elements.sent-2411-067: When visualizing the two-point correlation function, we observe a composition of multiple Einstein rings in the QG model, reflecting the spatial quantum superposition of the mass source.
elements.sent-2411-068: By contrast, the SN model produces a single deformed ring image, indicating a classical spacetime configuration.
elements.sent-2411-069: In addition, visualizing the which-path information indicator shows multiple Einstein rings in the QG model, while in the SN model, the image intensity notably disappears.
elements.sent-2411-070: Building on the foundational work of Feynmann and the BMV papers, this research serves as a relativistic extension, emphasizing the quantum nature of gravity beyond the Newton regime.
elements.sent-2411-071: From a perspective of gravitational lensing research, this study will clarify the quantum effects observable in the Einstein ring image.
elements.sent-2411-072: Although it presents significant challenges to verify these effects through actual observation, we believe this theoretical exploration is valuable, as it provides a practical formulation of the quantum field theory in non-dynamical curved spacetime induced by a superposed gravitational mass source and serves as a concrete example of how the quantum effect of gravity arises in general relativistic phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-073: The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: In Section~\ref{sec:Time_evolution , we detail our setup and calculate the time evolution of the total system, which includes the mass source of the background spacetime, the scalar field and the UDW detectors.}
elements.sent-2411-074: In Section~\ref{sec:Gravity_induced_entanglement , we evaluate the quantum entanglement between the mass source and other systems resulting from the QG interaction.}
elements.sent-2411-075: Section~\ref{sec:observer_accessible_quantity provides the reduced density matrix of the UDW detectors and introduces two specific quantities measurable by these detectors: the two-point correlation function of the scalar field and the which-path information indicator.}
elements.sent-2411-076: In Section~\ref{sec:imaging , we present the results of the Einstein ring image for both the QG and SN models.}
elements.sent-2411-077: Section~\ref{sec:discussion addresses advanced topics, including the experimental feasibility of our proposal, the discussions of an alternative semi-classical gravity model that induces gravitational state collapse, and connections to other research, particularly quantum reference frames~\cite{Zych2018,Chen2023,Giacomini2019,Foo2020,Foo2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Finally, we summarize our findings in Section~\ref{sec:conclusion .}
elements.section-2411-002: Scalar field and the Unruh-DeWitt detector in a curved spacetime induced by a superposed mass source
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.65\linewidth]{images/setup.png} \caption{Setup of our proposal: The background spacetime is a weak gravitational field generated by a mass source in spatial quantum superposition. A massless quantum scalar field propagates in this spacetime, and two Unruh-DeWitt detectors are coupled to the scalar field. The observer in the setup can only access the measurements obtained from these detectors. } \label{fig:setup}
elements.sent-2411-079: This section describes our setup and derives a time-evolved state of the total system.
elements.sent-2411-080: The schematic picture of our setup is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:setup .}
elements.sent-2411-081: We aim to investigate the gravitational lensing of light on a curved spacetime induced by a mass source in spatially quantum superposition.
elements.sent-2411-082: We do so by first considering the background spacetime induced by the first quantized mass source, described by the quantum state $|\mu\rangle_\text{M}$.
elements.sent-2411-083: We also suppose that the massless quantum scalar field $\hat \phi$ is a test field corresponding to the toy model of light propagating in the background spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-084: Furthermore, we clarify the measurement process of the scalar field by introducing two Unruh-DeWitt (UDW) detectors that couple to the scalar field and are placed sufficiently far from the mass source to ensure decoupling from the mass source system.
elements.sent-2411-085: In addition, we assume that an observer only has access to the measurement outcomes of these detectors.
elements.sent-2411-086: Therefore, our setup comprises four subsystems: the mass source system, the scalar field system, and two UDW detector systems named A and B.
elements.sent-2411-087: We first describe the background metric, the Hamiltonian, and the initial state, and finally, we show the time-evolved state of the total system.
elements.sent-2411-088: The background metric is supposed to be a weak gravitational field of a quantum point mass source with its mass $M$.
elements.sent-2411-089: If we denote the spacetime coordinate with $(t,x,y,z)$ and the mass source position operator with $\hat\X$, the background metric is given as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:metric} ds^2=g_{\mu\nu}dx^\mu dx^\nu =-\left(1+\frac{2\Phi^{(QG)/(SN)}}{c^2}\right)c^2dt^2+\left(1-\frac{2\Phi^{(QG)/(SN)}}{c^2}\right)d\x^2+O(1/c^4) .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-090: Here, $\Phi^{(\text{QG ) ,~\Phi^{(\text{SN )}$ are the Newtonian potentials for the quantized gravity model (QG) and the \schrodinger-Newton gravity model (SN) respectively: \begin{equation}\Phi^{(QG)}(\x,\hat\X)=\frac{-GM}{|\x-\hat \X|}, \label{eq:QG} \Phi^{(SN)}(\x)=\langle\mu| \frac{-GM}{|\x-\hat \X|} |\mu\rangle_M =\int d^3 X |\mu(\X)|^2 \frac{-GM}{|\x-\X|} , \label{eq:SN}\end{equation} where $\mu(\X)=\langle \X|\mu\rangle_\text{M}$ is the wave function of the mass source system.}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: Note that, unlike in Eqs.~\eqref{eq:intro_QG and \eqref{eq:intro_SN , the spatial coordinate describing the spacetime, $\x$, here is not in the operator form.}}
elements.sent-2411-092: This is because our setup considers a quantum scalar field $\hat\phi(t,\x)$ as the probe system, adopting a second quantization, while the original BMV papers~\cite{Bose2017,Marletto2017 employ a first-quantized particle as the probe system, represented as $\hat\x$.}
elements.sent-2411-093: Although our treatment of the probe differs slightly from the BMV approach, we will later confirm that this setup also produces gravity-induced entanglement between the scalar field probe and the mass source system.
elements.sent-2411-094: Next, let us provide a comparison of the physical interpretations of these two gravity models.
elements.sent-2411-095: The QG potential contains the position operator of the mass source system.
elements.sent-2411-096: This indicates that the spacetime inherits the quantum fluctuation of the mass source position, and that the spacetime itself is also quantum fluctuating.
elements.sent-2411-097: In the QG case, the mass source state $|\mu\rangle_\text{M}$ is not yet present at this stage, but will appear later when we compute the time-evolved state and various expectation values.
elements.sent-2411-098: However, the SN potential is given by the expectation value of the QG potential and does not directly depend on the mass source system operator, resulting in the metric being a c-number, unlike the QG metric.
elements.sent-2411-099: In other words, the SN potential can be viewed as a single classical entity represented by the ensemble-averaged form of the QG potential over various mass source positions $\X$, as indicated in the second equality of Eq.~\eqref{eq:SN .}
elements.sent-2411-100: Later, we will show the calculation for the QG model in section~\ref{sec:time_evolution_QG and the SN model in section~\ref{sec:time_evolution_SN , respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-101: On the background spacetime given in Eq.~\eqref{eq:metric , we consider the dynamics of the scalar field and two UDW detector systems.}
elements.sent-2411-102: The UDW detector is an idealized model of an atomic detector, described by a two-level qubit that changes its energy level owing to the interaction with the quantum field \cite{Unruh1976 .}
elements.sent-2411-103: This model helps to provide an operational way of measuring field quanta and is often analyzed in the context of the entanglement harvesting of the quantum field.
elements.sent-2411-104: The two UDW detectors in our setup are each assumed to couple independently to the massless quantum scalar field.
elements.sent-2411-105: The measurement process with UDW detectors clarifies the observable quantities in our setup, outlining specific steps for constructing the Einstein ring image from the observer-accessible quantities.
elements.sent-2411-106: Now, the Hamiltonian of the detectors and the scalar field is given as follows: \begin{equation}\hat H_{tot} =\hat H_0+\hat H_{int},\quad \hat H_0 =\hat H_A+\hat H_B+\hat H_{S} .\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}\hat H_{tot} =\hat H_0+\hat H_{int},\quad \hat H_0 =\hat H_A+\hat H_B+\hat H_{S} .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-107: The free evolution Hamiltonian of detector systems A and B is given by \begin{equation}\hat H_A=\frac{\hbar \omega_D}{2}\hat \sigma_{z,A},\quad \hat H_B=\frac{\hbar \omega_D}{2}\hat \sigma_{z,B},\end{equation} where $\hbar\omega_\text{D}$ is the internal energy gap of the UDW detector model and $\hat \sigma_{z,\text{A/B }$ are the $z$-component of the Pauli operators for detectors A/B.}
elements.sent-2411-108: These Hamiltonian indicates that detectors A/B are described by two states $|0\rangle_\text{A/B}$ or $|1\rangle_\text{A/B}$, whose energy eigenvalues are $+\hbar \omega_\text{D /2$ and $-\hbar \omega_\text{D /2$, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-109: Let us also introduce the free evolution Hamiltonian of the scalar field $\hat\phi$.

elements.sent-2411-116: We solve the time evolution of the total system in the interaction picture using the Hamiltonian given above.
elements.sent-2411-117: We show the formalism for the QG case in the next section and the SN case in Section \ref{sec:time_evolution_SN .}
elements.subsection-2411-001: Time evolution for the quantized gravity model
elements.sent-2411-118: In this section, let us solve the time evolution for the QG model, whose potential $\Phi^{(\text{QG ) (\x,\hat \X)$ is given in Eq.~\eqref{eq:QG .}}}
elements.sent-2411-119: Recalling that the QG spacetime depends on the mass source position operator $\hat\X$, we can see that the various physical quantities on this spacetime also depend on $\hat\X$.
elements.sent-2411-120: To emphasize this, we specify the mass source position dependence of each quantity in the QG case.
elements.sent-2411-121: For example, the scalar field Hamiltonian given in Eq.~\eqref{eq:Hamiltonian_scalar is revisited as follows \begin{equation}\label{eq:Hamiltonian_scalar_QG} \hat H_{S}(\hat \X) =\int d^3x \sqrt{g^{(3)}}\,\left[ -\frac{1}{2}g^{00}(\hat \X)(\partial_0 \hat\phi)^2 +\frac{1}{2}g^{ij}(\hat \X) \nabla_i \hat\phi \, \nabla_j \hat\phi \right].\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-122: Note that the scalar field Hamiltonian depends on the mass source position operator $\hat \X$ inside the background spacetime, which induces the quantum entanglement between the mass source and the scalar field systems.
elements.sent-2411-123: This is nothing but gravity-induced entanglement in a framework of quantum field theory in curved spacetime, as advocated in the BMV papers~\cite{Bose2017,Marletto2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-124: We move on to the interaction picture by considering $\hat H_0(\hat\X)=\hat H_\text{A +\hat H_\text{B +\hat H_{\text{S (\hat \X)$ as the solvable part and $\hat H_\text{int}$ as the interaction part of the total Hamiltonian.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-125: Then, the time-evolved state of the total system is written as \begin{equation}|\Psi^{(QG)}(t)\rangle = \hat U_I^{(\hat \X)}(t) |\Psi_ini\rangle,\end{equation} where the time evolution operator in the interaction picture is given as \begin{equation}\label{eq:time_evolution_operator} \hat U_I^{(\hat \X)}(t) =T\exp\left[-\frac{i}{\hbar}\int dt \hat H_I^{(\hat \X)}(t) \right],\end{equation} and the time-dependent interaction Hamiltonian in the interaction picture is \begin{equation}\label{eq:Hamiltonian_I} \hat H_I^{(\hat \X)}(t) = e^{i\hat H_0(\hat\X) t/\hbar}\,\hat H_{int}\,e^{-i\hat H_0(\hat\X) t/\hbar} =\lambda\, \chi(t) \sum_{j=A,B} \hat\sigma_{x,j}(t)\,\hat\phi^{(\hat \X)}(t,\x_j).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Hamiltonian_I} \hat H_I^{(\hat \X)}(t) = e^{i\hat H_0(\hat\X) t/\hbar}\,\hat H_{int}\,e^{-i\hat H_0(\hat\X) t/\hbar} =\lambda\, \chi(t) \sum_{j=A,B} \hat\sigma_{x,j}(t)\,\hat\phi^{(\hat \X)}(t,\x_j).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-126: The time-dependent operators of the detectors $\hat\sigma_{x,\text{A /\text{B (t)$ are driven straightforwardly as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Heisenberg_detector_operator} \hat\sigma_{x,j}(t) = \exp\left[\frac{i}{\hbar}\,\hat H_j\,t\right]\hat \sigma_{x,j}\exp\left[-\frac{i}{\hbar}\,\hat H_j\,t\right] =e^{-i\omega_D t}|0\rangle_j \,{}_j\langle 1| +e^{i\omega_D t}|1\rangle_j \,{}_j\langle 0|.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-127: Let us also derive the expression of the scalar field operator in the interaction picture $\hat\phi^{(\hat \X) (t,\x)$.}
elements.sent-2411-128: Although written earnestly as $\hat\phi^{(\hat \X) (t,\x_j)=e^{i\hat H_0(\hat\X) t/\hbar}\,\hat\phi(\x_j)\,e^{-i\hat H_0(\hat\X) t/\hbar}$, it is better to solve the Klein-Gordon equation of the free scalar field directly.}
elements.sent-2411-129: We do so by first performing an eigendecomposition of the scalar field operator $\hat \phi^{(\hat \X) (x)$ for the mass source position operator $\hat \X$ as follows \begin{equation}\label{eq:scalar_decomposition1} \hat \phi^{(\hat \X)}(x) =\int d^3X |\X\rangle\langle \X| \hat \phi^{(\X)}(x) .\end{equation}.}

elements.section-2411-003: Gravity-induced entanglement between the mass source and other systems
elements.sent-2411-213: In the previous section, we calculated the time evolution of the total system for two gravity models, QG and SN.
elements.sent-2411-214: In the QG spacetime, we see that the mass source system and other systems, the scalar field and two UDW detectors, entangle through gravitational interaction, as in Eq.~\eqref{eq:time_evolved_state .}
elements.sent-2411-215: This section quantitatively evaluates the production of the gravity-induced entanglement between the mass source and other systems for the QG case.
elements.sent-2411-216: At the end of this section, we also mention that the entanglement indicator goes to zero if we do the same calculation for the SN case.
elements.sent-2411-217: Specifically, we use linear entropy as an indicator of quantum entanglement.
elements.sent-2411-218: First, let us define the linear entropy for a general setup, denoting an arbitrary subsystem as S and its complement system as $\Bar{\text{S }$.}
elements.sent-2411-219: The total system is represented by the combination of two systems S and $\Bar{\text{S }$, and we assume that it is a pure state.}

elements.section-2411-004: Observer-accessible quantities using the Unruh-DeWitt detectors

elements.subsection-2411-003: Reduced density matrix and the observables of the UDW detectors
elements.sent-2411-242: Here, let us show the explicit form of the reduced density matrix of two UDW detectors A and B.
elements.sent-2411-243: We will first discuss the QG case, followed by a brief examination of the SN case.

elements.formula-2411-049: \begin{equation}M_AB^{(\X)}=  1-\sum_{j=A,B} E^{(\X)}(\x_j,\x_j) 0 0 X^{(\X)}(\x_A,\x_B) 0 E^{(\X)}(\x_B,\x_B) E^{(\X)}(\x_A,\x_B) 0 0 E^{(\X)}(\x_B,\x_A) E^{(\X)}(\x_A,\x_A) 0 X^{(\X)}(\x_A,\x_B)^* 0 0 0  , \label{eq:detector_matrix} E^{(\X)}(\x_i,\x_j) =\frac{\lambda^2}{\hbar^2} \iint dt dt'\, e^{i\omega_D(t-t')}\,\chi(t)\chi(t')\, \left\langle \hat\phi^{(\X)}(t,\x_i)\hat\phi^{(\X)}(t,\x_j) \right\rangle , X^{(\X)}(\x_i,\x_j) =-\frac{\lambda^2}{\hbar^2} \iint dt dt'\, e^{i\omega_D(t+t')}\,\chi(t)\chi(t') \notag \times\left\{ \theta(t'-t) \left\langle \hat\phi^{(\X)}(t,\x_i)\hat\phi^{(\X)}(t',\x_j) \right\rangle + \theta(t-t') \left\langle \hat\phi^{(\X)}(t',\x_j)\hat\phi^{(\X)}(t,\x_i) \right\rangle \right\} .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-246: Similarly, we obtain the reduced density matrix of two detectors for the SN case as follows: \begin{equation}\hat\rho_AB(t,\x_A,\x_B) =Tr_MS\left[ |\Psi^{(SN)}(t)\rangle\langle \Psi^{(SN)}(t)| \right] =\sum_{m,n=1}^4\left(M_AB^{(SN)}\right)_{mn} |e_m\rangle \langle e_n| .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-247: Here, the detector bases are the same as in Eq.~\eqref{eq:detector_basis , and the matrix components also remains the same as in Eq.~\eqref{eq:detector_matrix , except that we have replaced the scalar field operator $\hat\phi^{(\X)}$ with that corresponding to the SN case, $\hat\phi^{(\text{SN )}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-248: Each matrix component of the detector state, $\mathcal{M _\text{AB ^{(\X)}$ for the QG case or $\mathcal{M _\text{AB ^{(\text{SN )}$ for the SN case, is accessible to the observer by measuring the expectation value of the Pauli operators of detectors A and B.}}}}}

elements.subsection-2411-004: Two-point correlation function of the scalar field as an observer-accessible quantity
elements.sent-2411-254: The scalar field correlation function(CF) is often used to analyze the wave optical image in curved spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-255: In previous study~\cite{Kanai2013,Nambu2013,Nambu2016 , the authors analyzed the Einstein ring image using a Fourier analysis of the classical scalar field solution, corresponding to the mode function of the scalar field operator in our case.}
elements.sent-2411-256: In addition, in a previous study~\cite{Nambu2022 , the authors investigated the wave optical image of Hawking radiation by calculating the quantum scalar field emitted from a classical black hole and applying a Fourier analysis to its two-point CF.}
elements.sent-2411-257: In this section, we also introduce the two-point CF of the scalar field operator, which is instrumental in studying the wave optical image, eventually revealing the Einstein ring and providing rich information about the background spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-258: We also show that the introduced CF is accessible to the observer.
elements.sent-2411-259: We define the scalar field CF as follows: \begin{equation}Q_CF(\x_A,\x_B) :=\frac{1}{2\pi\,\theta(\omega_D)}\int_{-\infty}^\infty d(t-t')~ e^{i\omega_D (t-t')} \langle\Psi_ini| \hat\phi(t,\x_A)\, \hat\phi(t',\x_B) |\Psi_ini\rangle . \label{eq:CF}\end{equation} Using the mode expansion of the scalar field solution, given in Eqs.~\eqref{eq:scalar_decomposition1 ,~\eqref{eq:scalar_decomposition2 for the QG case and Eq.~\eqref{eq:scalar_decomposition_SN for the SN case, we can derive the explicit expression of the scalar field CF.}}}
elements.sent-2411-260: Furthermore, by assuming $\chi(t)=1$, the CF can be simplified as follows: \begin{equation}Q_CF(\x_A,\x_B) = \begin{dcases} \quad \int d^3X |\mu(\X)|^2 \varphi^{(\X)}(\omega_D,\x_A) \varphi^{(\X)}(\omega_D,\x_B)^* \quad(The QG case) , \quad \varphi^{(SN)}(\omega_D,\x_A) \varphi^{(SN)}(\omega_D,\x_B)^* \quad(The SN case) . \end{dcases} \label{eq:CF_QG_SN}\end{equation} This shows the correlation between the scalar fields at different spatial positions $\x_\text{A}$ and $\x_\text{B}$.
elements.sent-2411-261: Compared with Eq.~\eqref{eq:observable_expectation_value , CF can be rewritten using the expectation value of the detector observable $\hat{\mathcal{O _\text{AB}$ \begin{equation}Q_CF(\x_A,\x_B) =\left(\left(\int_{-\infty}^\infty dt_+\right) \frac{\pi\lambda^2}{\hbar^2} \theta(\omega_D)\right)^{-1} \times~ Tr\left[ \hat{O}_AB \,\hat \rho_AB(t,\x_A,\x_B) \right] .\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-262: Therefore, the observer can reproduce the scalar field CF by performing $\hat{\mathcal{O _\text{AB}$ measurement.}}
elements.sent-2411-263: Subsequently, in Section~\ref{sec:imaging , we will present the wave optical image obtained by applying the imaging process to CF, which reveals the Einstein ring containing rich information on the background spacetime.}
elements.subsection-2411-005: Which-path information indicator as an observer-accessible quantity
elements.sent-2411-264: In this section, we introduce another quantity, named the which-path information (WPI) indicator: \begin{equation}\label{eq:WPI} Q_WPI(\x_A,\x_B) :=Q_CF(\x_A,\x_B) -\frac{1}{2\pi\theta(\omega_D)}\int_{-\infty}^\infty d(t-t')~ e^{i\omega_D (t-t')} \langle\Psi_ini| \hat\phi(t,\x_A) |\mu\rangle_M\,{}_M\langle\mu| \hat\phi(t',\x_B) |\Psi_ini\rangle .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-265: We highlight two features motivating the use of the WPI indicator: First, the WPI indicator contains the two-point CF $\mathcal{Q _\text{CF (\x_\text{A ,\x_\text{B )$ introduced in the previous section.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-266: As discussed in the previous section, CF is commonly used to examine the wave optical images in curved spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-267: Later, by applying a Fourier analysis to the WPI indicator, we obtain the WPI indicator image which reveals the Einstein ring, as in the CF image.
elements.sent-2411-268: More importantly, the second feature of the WPI indicator is that it is more sensitive to the gravity model (QG or SN) than the two-point CF.
elements.sent-2411-269: By substituting the scalar field solution obtained in Section~\ref{sec:Time_evolution and assuming $\chi(t)=1$, we obtain the explicit form of the WPI indicator.}
elements.sent-2411-270: For the QG case, it simplifies to \begin{equation}\label{eq:WPI_QG} Q_WPI(\x_A,\x_B) =\frac{1}{2}\int d^3X\,d^3X' |\mu(\X)|^2 |\mu(\X')|^2\notag \times \left(\varphi^{(\X)}(\omega_D,\x_A)-\varphi^{(\X')}(\omega_D,\x_A)\right) \left(\varphi^{(\X)}(\omega_D,\x_B)^*-\varphi^{(\X')}(\omega_D,\x_B)^*\right),\end{equation} while for the SN case, we obtain \begin{equation}\label{eq:WPI_SN} Q_WPI(\x_A,\x_B)=0 .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-271: Hence, the WPI indicator becomes zero in the SN case, which is an interesting feature not seen in the CF.
elements.sent-2411-272: Now, let us explain that the WPI indicator is closely related to the which-path information, as its name suggests, and is consequently linked to the gravity-induced entanglement.
elements.sent-2411-273: According to the WPI indicator for the QG case given in Eq.~\eqref{eq:WPI_QG , it considers the difference between two scalar field mode functions $\varphi^{(\X) (\omega_\text{D ,\x_\text{A/B )-\varphi^{(\X') (\omega_\text{D ,\x_\text{A/B )$; one is scattered by the mass source at $\X$, and the other by the mass source at $\X'$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-274: This implies how much the scalar field changes in its time evolution depending on the mass source position.
elements.sent-2411-275: Hence, this quantity was named the WPI indicator.
elements.sent-2411-276: However, in the SN case, the scalar field evolves uniformly, unaffected by the mass source position.
elements.sent-2411-277: Consequently, the field does not acquire which-path information about the mass source, consistent with Eq.~\eqref{eq:WPI_SN .}
elements.sent-2411-278: In particular, when the mass source is in a \schrodinger~cat state as in Eq.~\eqref{eq:BMV_mass_source , the time evolution in the QG and SN spacetime follows the patterns sketched in Figs.~\ref{fig:QGevolution and \ref{fig:SNevolution , respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-279: In the QG case, the scalar field evolves differently depending on whether the mass source is on the right or the left.
elements.sent-2411-280: The blue and red arrows represent the distinct propagations of the scalar field with respect to the position of the mass source, and the WPI indicator, given in Eq.~\eqref{eq:WPI_QG , quantifies the difference between these two propagations.}
elements.sent-2411-281: Conversely, in the SN case, the background spacetime is represented by a single ensemble-averaged configuration, regardless of the specific position of the mass source.
elements.sent-2411-282: Consequently, the propagation of the scalar field follows a single pattern, as depicted by the purple arrows, and the WPI indicator becomes trivial, as in Eq.~\eqref{eq:WPI_SN .}
elements.sent-2411-283: Now, let us discuss the connection between the WPI indicator and gravity-induced entanglement, which is evaluated using the linear entropy of the mass source system, as described in Eq.~\eqref{eq:GIE_simplified .}
elements.sent-2411-284: We first propose the following logical expression linking the linear entropy and WPI indicator: \begin{equation}\label{eq:logical_E_Q} E(\x_A,\x_B)=0 \quad\rightarrow\quad Q_WPI(\x_A,\x_B)=0 .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-285: The derivation is given in Appendix~\ref{apdx:WPI .}
elements.sent-2411-286: This expression indicates that, if no gravity-induced entanglement occurs, for example, when the mass source is not in the spatial superposition or when the SN model is applied, the WPI indicator goes 0.
elements.sent-2411-287: Taking the contrapositive, the non-zero value of the WPI indicator directly confirms the existence of gravity-induced entanglement.
elements.sent-2411-288: Note that for a specific case, the WPI indicator vanishes even if no gravity-induced entanglement is produced; the reverse of the logical expression given in Eq.~\eqref{eq:logical_E_Q does not necessarily hold.}
elements.sent-2411-289: Let us show the example for the mass source in a \schrodinger~cat state of the left and right positions as in Eq.~\eqref{eq:BMV_mass_source .}
elements.sent-2411-290: Gravity-induced entanglement is generally produced in the QG case.
elements.sent-2411-291: In most cases, the detectors observe different scalar field propagations depending on the position of the mass source, leading to a non-zero value of the WPI indicator, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:non-zeroWPI .}
elements.sent-2411-292: However, if detector A is particularly positioned at the same distance from the left and right mass source states, specifically satisfying $|\x_\text{A -\X_\text{L |=|\x_\text{A -\X_\text{R |$ and $z_\text{A -Z_\text{L =z_\text{A -Z_\text{R}$, the WPI indicator disappears, as shown in Fig.~\eqref{fig:zeroWPI .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-293: Two propagations of the scalar field are observed, one scattered by the left mass source (blue arrow) and another scattered by the right mass source (red arrow), which appear identical except for a parity inversion from the perspective of detector A.
elements.sent-2411-294: Then, detector A measures the scalar field locally, the value of which is identical for the two different field propagations.
elements.sent-2411-295: Therefore, cases may exist in which the WPI indicator vanishes, even though gravity-induced entanglement is generally produced, depending on the detector arrangement.
elements.figure-2411-004: \begin{minipage}{0.49\linewidth} \includegraphics[scale=0.3]{images/nonzeroWPI.png} \subcaption{ Nonsymmetric detector positioning resulting in a non-zero value of the WPI indicator.} \label{fig:non-zeroWPI} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.49\linewidth} \includegraphics[scale=0.3]{images/zeroWPI.png} \subcaption{Symmetric detector positioning that results in a vanishing WPI indicator.} \label{fig:zeroWPI} \end{minipage} \caption{ The existence of the WPI indicator in the QG spacetime depends on the detector arrangement. However, for a specific detector arrangement, the WPI indicator vanishes as shown in the right panel. }
elements.sent-2411-296: Moreover, if we consider the linear entropy for the same detector positions $\x_\text{A =\x_\text{B}$, we obtain the following logical expression: \begin{equation}\label{eq:logical_E_Q_2} E(\x_A,\x_A)=0 \quad\leftrightarrow\quad Q_WPI(\x_A,\x_B)=0 \quadfor~~\forall\,\x_B .\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-298: The expression suggests that observing the zero/non-zero value of the WPI $\x_B$ serves as proof of the non-existence/existence of gravity-induced entanglement, at least for the same detector position $\x_\text{A =\x_\text{B}$.}
elements.sent-2411-299: In summary, the two features of the WPI indicator show that it can be used to generate wave optical images, providing insights into the background curved spacetime, while also serving as a witness of gravity-induced entanglement.
elements.sent-2411-300: Later, in Section \ref{sec:imaging , we will perform a Fourier analysis of the WPI indicator to generate its wave optical image, allowing us to visualize the entanglement production induced by gravity as if projected onto the celestial sphere of the observer.}
elements.sent-2411-301: Finally, we show how the WPI indicator can be rewritten in terms of the detector observable and accessible to the observer.
elements.sent-2411-302: Here, we show the driven relational formula of the WPI indicator and the detector observable holding for the QG and SN cases in the following: \begin{equation}Q_WPI(\x_A,\x_B) \simeq\left(\left(\int_{-\infty}^\infty dt_+\right) \frac{\pi\lambda^2}{\hbar^2} \theta(\omega_D)\right)^{-1}\notag \times~ \left( Tr\left[\hat{O}_AB\, \hat\rho(t,\x_A,\x_B)\right] -\int d^3 X |\mu(\X)|^2 \, Tr\left[\hat{O}_AB\, \hat\rho(t,\x_A,\x_B^{(falling)}-\X)\right] \right). \label{eq:WPI_observable}\end{equation} Here, $\x_\text{B ^{(\text{falling )}$ is a detector position that feels the background field and changes its evolution depending on the gravity model: \begin{equation}\label{eq:xB_falling} \x_B^{(falling)} = \begin{dcases} \x_B+\hat \X (The QG case) , \x_B+\langle\hat \X\rangle_M (The SN case) . \end{dcases} .\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-303: We used the translational covariance property of the scalar field mode function given in Eq.~\eqref{eq:translation to derive Eq.~\eqref{eq:WPI_observable for the QG case.}}
elements.sent-2411-304: In addition, for both the QG and SN cases, we used the expectation value of the detector observable $\hat{\mathcal{O _\text{AB}$ provided in Eq.~\eqref{eq:observable_expectation_value .}}}

elements.section-2411-005: Einstein ring image in the weak gravitational field induced by the superposed mass source
elements.sent-2411-313: In this section, we generate Einstein ring images in the weak gravitational field induced by the spatially superposed mass source by applying an imaging process to the observer-accessible quantities, namely the two-point correlation function introduced in Section~\ref{sec:CF and the which-path information indicator in Section~\ref{sec:WPI .}}

elements.sent-2411-317: In the previous section, we considered the observer who can only access the UDW detectors coupled to the propagating scalar field and introduced the observer-accessible quantities, the two-point CF of the scalar field $\mathcal{Q _\text{CF (\x_\text{A ,\x_\text{B )$ and the WPI indicator $\mathcal{Q _\text{WPI (\x_\text{A ,\x_\text{B )$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-318: In this section, we collectively denote such observer-accessible quantities as $\mathcal{Q (\x_\text{A ,\x_\text{B )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-319: Recall that this study aims to observe the Einstein ring image on a curved spacetime induced by a superposed mass source.
elements.sent-2411-320: For this purpose, let us explain how the observer builds the image using the given data for these quantities $\mathcal{Q (\x_\text{A ,\x_\text{B )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-321: The imaging process involves two primary steps.
elements.sent-2411-322: The observer prepares the two-dimensional array data of the observer-accessible quantity by repeating the measurement while changing the detector position.
elements.sent-2411-323: The Fourier analysis of the prepared two-dimensional array data is performed, and two-dimensional image intensity data is generated.
elements.sent-2411-324: We explain the details of each step as follows: As a first step, let us demonstrate how the observer prepares the two-dimensional array data of the observer-accessible quantity $Q(\x_\text{A ,\x_\text{B )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-325: As depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:imaging_process , we consider keeping detector A fixed on the $z$-axis, while moving the detector B in the $x,y$-direction \begin{equation}\label{eq:detector_position} \x_A=(0,0,z_D), \quad \x_B=(x_B,y_B,z_D) .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-326: Note that $z_\text{D}$ is a fixed constant.
elements.sent-2411-327: Repetition of the measurement on $Q(\x_\text{A ,\x_\text{B )$ for various positions of $x_\text{B}$ and $y_\text{B}$, the observer can collect data of the two-dimensional array of $Q(\x_\text{A ,\x_\text{B )$ in the $(x_\text{B ,y_\text{B )$ plane.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-328: Specifically, in this study, we consider moving detector B within the disc area on the $(x,\,y)$-plane centered at $(0,0,z_\text{D )$ and having radius $\ell$ as shown by a light blue disk in Fig.~\ref{fig:imaging_process .}}
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=0.45\linewidth]{images/setup_image_formation.png} \caption{The imaging process setup using two UDW detectors.} \label{fig:imaging_process}
elements.sent-2411-329: As a second step, let us perform the Fourier analysis of the two-dimensional array data of $Q(\x_\text{A ,\x_\text{B )$ obtained in the first step and build its image \begin{equation}\label{eq:imaging_formula} \tilde{Q}(\x_I) := \int_{|\x_B-\x_A|\leq \ell} d^2(\x_B-\x_A) ~ e^{i\omega_D(\x_B-\x_A) \cdot \x_I/(cf)} \, Q(\x_A,\x_B) = \iint_{\sqrt{x_B^2+y_B^2}\leq \ell} dx_B \, dy_B ~ e^{i\omega_D(x_Bx_I+y_By_I)/(cf)} \, Q(\x_A,\x_B) .\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-064: \begin{equation}\label{eq:imaging_formula} \tilde{Q}(\x_I) := \int_{|\x_B-\x_A|\leq \ell} d^2(\x_B-\x_A) ~ e^{i\omega_D(\x_B-\x_A) \cdot \x_I/(cf)} \, Q(\x_A,\x_B) = \iint_{\sqrt{x_B^2+y_B^2}\leq \ell} dx_B \, dy_B ~ e^{i\omega_D(x_Bx_I+y_By_I)/(cf)} \, Q(\x_A,\x_B) .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-330: Here, $\x_I=(x_I,y_I)$ is the two-dimensional coordinate of the Fourier domain, and $f$ is a constant in length units.
elements.sent-2411-331: We refer to the quantity $\tilde{\mathcal{Q (\x_I)$, the spectrum of the given data $\mathcal{Q (\x_\text{A ,\x_\text{B )$, as an image intensity.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-332: Let us mention why the Fourier transformation procedure in Eq.~\eqref{eq:imaging_formula explains the imaging process and the spectrum $\tilde{\mathcal{Q (\x_I)$ can be interpreted as an image intensity.}}}
elements.sent-2411-333: The operation in Eq.~\eqref{eq:imaging_formula is motivated by the lens diffraction\footnote{ Note that the lens in the context of wave optics is unrelated to gravitational lensing.}}
elements.sent-2411-334: In wave optics, the Fourier transformation is used to explain the behavior of the wave as it is diffracted by an optical lens and projected onto a screen at the focal point.
elements.sent-2411-335: In this context, $f$ denotes the focal length of the lens, $\ell$ represents the lens aperture, and $(x_I,\,y_I)$ corresponds to the coordinates on the screen.
elements.sent-2411-336: Therefore, the operation in Eq.~\eqref{eq:imaging_formula can be interpreted as representing how the incident wave $\mathcal{Q (\x_\text{A ,\x_\text{B )$ would appear as an image when observed through a telescope.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-337: If we observe the incident wave $\mathcal{Q (\x_\text{A ,\x_\text{B )$ directly without performing this operation, we cannot confirm a clear image like the Einstein ring.}}}
elements.sent-2411-338: Further details about the image formation method in wave optics are given in Appendix~\ref{apdx:image_formation .}
elements.sent-2411-339: By substituting each observer-accessible quantity, such as the CF and the WPI indicator, into Eq.~\eqref{eq:imaging_formula , we obtain the corresponding image intensity.}
elements.sent-2411-340: The Fourier integral in Eq.~\eqref{eq:imaging_formula is performed numerically.}
elements.sent-2411-341: In the next section, we will show the numerical result of the image intensity for each observer-accessible quantity.
elements.sent-2411-342: Especially, in Section~\ref{sec:classical_spacetime_image , we demonstrate the results for the classical spacetime to describe the fundamental feature of the image.}
elements.sent-2411-343: In Section~\ref{sec:superposed_spacetime_image , we finally show our main results for the spacetime induced by the superposed mass source, specifically the QG and SN spacetime.}
elements.subsection-2411-007: Images in the classical curved spacetime
elements.sent-2411-344: In this section, we show the image intensity results on a classical curved spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-345: Let us suppose that the quantum state of the point mass source $|\mu\rangle_\text{M}$ is localized at some constant position $\X_\text{M}$ as follows: \begin{equation}|\mu(\X)|^2 = \delta^{(3)}(\X-\X_M) .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-346: Then, the background spacetime is regarded as classical in the sense that the mass source has no spatial quantum fluctuation.
elements.sent-2411-347: Note that the QG and SN models do not need to be considered here since the mass source and background spacetime are both treated classically.
elements.sent-2411-348: Following the imaging process given in the previous section, we obtain the CF image intensity in the classical curved spacetime as follows: \begin{equation}\tilde{Q}_CF(\x_I) :=\int_{|\x_B-\x_A|\leq \ell} d^2(\x_B-\x_A) ~ Q_CF(\x_A,\x_B) \, e^{-i\frac{\omega_B}{c} \x_B \cdot \x_I/f} = \varphi^{(\X_M)}(\omega_D,\x_A) \iint_{\sqrt{x_B^2+y_B^2}\leq \ell} dx_B \, dy_B ~ e^{i\omega_D(x_Bx_I+y_By_I)/(cf)} \varphi^{(\X_M)}(\omega_D,\x_B)^* . \label{eq:CF_image_classical}\end{equation} This shows that the CF image intensity is determined by the Fourier spectrum of the scalar field mode function $\varphi^{(\X_\text{M ) (\omega_\text{D ,\x_\text{B )^*$, analogous to the Einstein ring image analysis in classical wave optics~\cite{Kanai2013,Nambu2016,Nambu2019 .}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-066: \begin{equation}\tilde{Q}_CF(\x_I) :=\int_{|\x_B-\x_A|\leq \ell} d^2(\x_B-\x_A) ~ Q_CF(\x_A,\x_B) \, e^{-i\frac{\omega_B}{c} \x_B \cdot \x_I/f} = \varphi^{(\X_M)}(\omega_D,\x_A) \iint_{\sqrt{x_B^2+y_B^2}\leq \ell} dx_B \, dy_B ~ e^{i\omega_D(x_Bx_I+y_By_I)/(cf)} \varphi^{(\X_M)}(\omega_D,\x_B)^* . \label{eq:CF_image_classical}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-349: In contrast to the CF image, the WPI indicator image vanishes trivially in the classical curved spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-350: This is owing to the absence of gravity-induced entanglement in the curved spacetime, which causes the WPI indicator to be zero, as can be seen from Eq.~\eqref{eq:logical_E_Q .}
elements.sent-2411-351: Hereafter, we define the parameter with length dimensions, $c/\omega_\text{D}$, as 1.
elements.sent-2411-352: Other parameters, such as $\x_\text{A ,~\x_\text{B}$, and so on, used in the subsequent numerical results are normalized to this unit length scale.}

elements.sent-2411-363: In the figure, we observe a ring image, corresponding to the peak of the spectrum for a typical oscillation frequency shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:imageCFaxclassical_CF .}
elements.sent-2411-364: This ring, known as the Einstein ring, arises from the diffraction of the massless scalar field caused by the curved spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-365: The Einstein ring radius $R_I$, indicated by a green dashed line, and its variance $\delta R_I$, shown by a blue arrow, are approximately given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:Einstein_ring_radius} R_I=\sqrt{\frac{2c\gamma}{(z_D-Z_M)\omega_D}}, \qquad \delta R_I = \frac{1}{z_D-Z_M}\left(\ell-\frac{3\pi c}{\omega_D}\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-366: In addition, when the mass source is positioned off the $z$-axis, the center of the Einstein ring shifts correspondingly on the $(x_I/f, y_I/f)$ plane, tracking the mass source position as \begin{equation}\label{eq:Einstein_ring_center} O_I = \frac{1}{2(z_D-Z_M)}\times (X_M,Y_M) .\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-369: In addition, the image reveals an interference fringe around the peak of the Einstein ring, of whose period is characterized by the scalar field frequency $\omega_\text{D}$, and the movable radius of detector B $\ell$ as \begin{equation}\label{eq:Einstein_ring_fringe} \frac{\omega_D \ell}{c} \times \frac{|\x_I|}{f} =2\pi n,\end{equation} where $n$ is an arbitrary integer.

elements.subsection-2411-008: Images in the curved spacetime induced by the superposed mass source
elements.sent-2411-394: This section investigates the Einstein ring image in the curved spacetime generated by the superposed mass source, examining the QG and SN spacetime, which is the primary aim of this study.
elements.sent-2411-395: Before showing the numerical results, we first outline the explicit form of the image intensity derived from the CF data and the WPI indicator data.
elements.sent-2411-396: Using Eqs.~\eqref{eq:CF_QG_SN , \eqref{eq:imaging_formula , the CF image intensity $\tilde{\mathcal{Q _\text{CF (\x_I)$ is given by \begin{equation}\tilde{Q}_CF(\x_I) :=\int_{|\x_B-\x_A|\leq \ell} d^2(\x_B-\x_A) ~ Q_CF(\x_A,\x_B) \, e^{-i\frac{\omega_B}{c} (\x_B-\x_A) \cdot \x_I/f} \notag = \begin{dcases} ~ \int d^3X |\mu(\X)|^2 \, \varphi^{(\X)}(\omega_D,\x_A) \iint_{\sqrt{x_B^2+y_B^2}\leq \ell} dx_B \, dy_B ~ e^{i\omega_D(x_Bx_I+y_By_I)/(cf)} \varphi^{(\X)}(\omega_D,\x_B)^* \quad(The QG case) , ~ \varphi^{(SN)}(\omega_D,\x_A) \iint_{\sqrt{x_B^2+y_B^2}\leq \ell} dx_B \, dy_B ~ e^{i\omega_D(x_Bx_I+y_By_I)/(cf)} \varphi^{(SN)}(\omega_D,\x_B)^* \quad(The SN case) . \end{dcases} \label{eq:CF_image_QG_SN}\end{equation} For the QG case, the expression suggests that we need to sum the Fourier spectrum of $\varphi^{(\X) (\omega_\text{D ,\x_\text{B )^*$ over the different positions of the mass source $\X$, using the mass source probability distribution $|\mu(\X)|^2$ as a weighting factor.}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-070: \begin{equation}\tilde{Q}_CF(\x_I) :=\int_{|\x_B-\x_A|\leq \ell} d^2(\x_B-\x_A) ~ Q_CF(\x_A,\x_B) \, e^{-i\frac{\omega_B}{c} (\x_B-\x_A) \cdot \x_I/f} \notag = \begin{dcases} ~ \int d^3X |\mu(\X)|^2 \, \varphi^{(\X)}(\omega_D,\x_A) \iint_{\sqrt{x_B^2+y_B^2}\leq \ell} dx_B \, dy_B ~ e^{i\omega_D(x_Bx_I+y_By_I)/(cf)} \varphi^{(\X)}(\omega_D,\x_B)^* \quad(The QG case) , ~ \varphi^{(SN)}(\omega_D,\x_A) \iint_{\sqrt{x_B^2+y_B^2}\leq \ell} dx_B \, dy_B ~ e^{i\omega_D(x_Bx_I+y_By_I)/(cf)} \varphi^{(SN)}(\omega_D,\x_B)^* \quad(The SN case) . \end{dcases} \label{eq:CF_image_QG_SN}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-397: This signifies that the image intensity in the QG spacetime is obtained by summing multiple classical Einstein ring images for the mass source located at various positions $\X$.
elements.sent-2411-398: More broadly, the image intensity becomes blurred owing to the quantum fluctuation of the mass source position, suggesting quantum dephasing or quantum decoherence of the image.
elements.sent-2411-399: Conversely, the SN case involves a single Fourier spectrum of $\varphi^{(\text{SN ) (\omega_\text{D ,\x_\text{B )^*$, and there is no need to sum over mass source positions, which means image decoherence does not occur, unlike the QG case.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-512: Finally, we discuss the connection to the concept of the quantum reference frame~\cite{Zych2018,Giacomini2019,Kabel2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-513: Quantum reference frames extend classical coordinate transformations by replacing the classical transformation parameter with a quantum operator.

elements.sent-2411-524: This final expression represents the superposition of the UDW detectors A and B in a purely classical background spacetime induced by the point mass source sharply localized at the origin, with the entire system subject to a quantum-extended translation transformation based on $\hat\x_\text{A}$.
elements.sent-2411-525: Thus, by applying the concept of the quantum reference frame, our analysis in the QG spacetime can be reinterpreted as a study of superposed UDW detectors in a classical background spacetime, as explored in a previous study~\cite{Foo2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-526: This study investigated gravitational lensing and the Einstein ring image resulting from a spatially superposed mass source.
elements.sent-2411-527: We considered a weak gravitational field characterized by two types of Newtonian potential: the quantized gravity (QG) model, which produces quantum entanglement between the mass source and probe system, and the \schrodinger-Newton (SN) gravity model, a semi-classical gravity model that does not produce entanglement.
elements.sent-2411-528: We treated a massless quantum scalar field as a toy model for light propagation in this background spacetime and introduced an observer utilizing two Unruh-DeWitt (UDW) detectors coupled to the scalar field (see Fig.~\ref{fig:setup ).}
elements.sent-2411-529: In Section~\ref{sec:observer_accessible_quantity , we defined two quantities accessible to the observer through UDW detector measurements: the two-point correlation function (CF) of the scalar field and the which-path information (WPI) indicator.}
elements.sent-2411-530: In particular, the WPI indicator was useful for witnessing gravitational entanglement, as shown in Eqs.~\eqref{eq:logical_E_Q and \eqref{eq:logical_E_Q_2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-531: Applying the imaging process detailed in Section~\ref{sec:image_formation to these observer-accessible quantities, we obtained the Einstein ring image in the curved spacetime induced by the superposed mass source.}
elements.sent-2411-532: The resulting images are shown in Figs.~\ref{fig:imageaxCFWPI_QG_SN and \ref{fig:imagenonsymCFWPI_QG_SN .}}
elements.sent-2411-533: In the case of the QG model, the images exhibited the composition of multiple Einstein rings, which can be interpreted as a manifestation of quantum decoherence.
elements.sent-2411-534: By contrast, in the SN model, the CF image revealed a single deformed Einstein ring, while the WPI image showed no intensity.
elements.sent-2411-535: Consequently, our findings offer a visual method for distinguishing the two gravity models through the Einstein ring images.
elements.sent-2411-536: In particular, the WPI image highlights a striking difference depending on the existence of gravity-induced entanglement.
elements.sent-2411-537: They reveal the composition of multiple Einstein ring images if and only if gravitational entanglement exists and otherwise vanishes.
elements.sent-2411-538: In Section~\ref{sec:discussion , we note that our analysis presents greater experimental challenges compared to proposals to test the entanglement produced by Newtonian gravity~\cite{Bose2017,Marletto2017 , as we explore their relativistic extensions at higher energy levels.}}
elements.sent-2411-539: Nevertheless, our proposal holds significant theoretical value, as it offers a practical formulation of the quantum field theory in non-dynamical curved spacetime induced by superposed mass source, and specifically presents how quantum effects of gravity arise in general relativistic phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-540: Several avenues for future work can be considered.
elements.sent-2411-541: First, we can explore applications to alternative semi-classical gravity models beyond the SN model, particularly those exhibiting the gravitational state collapse of the probe state, as briefly discussed in Section~\ref{sec:discussion .}
elements.sent-2411-542: In addition, investigating applications to other general relativistic phenomena, such as Shapiro time delay and frame dragging, would be interesting.
elements.sent-2411-543: Expanding our focus to different types of background curved spacetiems, especially strong gravitational fields like black holes spacetimes, as examined in \cite{Foo2022 , opens the door to even richer phenomena, including quasi-normal modes, photon spheres, and the Penrose process.}

elements.sent-2411-545: In this section, we show how to derive the mode function solution in the quantized gravity (QG) and \schrodinger-Newton (SN) spacetime.
elements.subsection-2411-009: Coulomb wave function as the mode function solution in the QG spacetime
elements.sent-2411-546: Recall that the quantized gravity (QG) spacetime is described by the weak gravitational field with the following Newtonian potential, as given in Eq.~\eqref{eq:QG : \begin{equation}\Phi^{(QG)}(\x,\hat\X)=\frac{-GM}{|\x-\hat \X|} .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-547: The mode function of the scalar field operator in the QG spacetime follows the \schrodinger-like equation given in Eq.~\eqref{eq:Schrodinger_eq .}
elements.sent-2411-548: First, let us focus on the case where the mass source is placed at the origin $\X=0$.
elements.sent-2411-549: The \schrodinger-like equation of the mode function is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:schrodinger_equation_2} \left[ \left(\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\right)^2+\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial y}\right)^2+\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial z}\right)^2 + \frac{\omega^2}{c^2}\left(1+\frac{4GM}{c^2 |\x|}\right) \right] \varphi^{(0)}(\omega,\x)=0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-550: By introducing the new coordinate $\xi:=|\x|+z,~\eta=|\x|-z,~\phi=\arctan(y/x)$, the \schrodinger-like equation can be rewritten as \begin{equation}\label{eq:schrodinger_equation_3} \left[ \frac{\partial}{\partial\xi}\left(\xi\frac{\partial}{\partial \xi}\right) +\frac{1}{4\xi}\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial \phi}\right)^2 +\frac{\omega^2}{4c^2} \xi +\frac{\partial}{\partial\eta}\left(\eta\frac{\partial}{\partial \eta}\right) +\frac{1}{4\eta}\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial \phi}\right)^2 +\frac{\omega^2}{4^2} \eta +\frac{\gamma \, \omega}{c} \right] \varphi^{(0)}(\omega,\x)=0,\end{equation} where $\gamma := 2GM\omega/c^3$ is a dimensionless gravitational coupling constant.
elements.sent-2411-551: We now omit the $\phi$-derivative term due to the axisymmetry of the system.
elements.sent-2411-552: Since the $\xi$ and $\eta$ dependence in the above equation is separable, the general solution of $\varphi^{(0) (\omega,\x)$ is given by \begin{equation}\varphi^{(0)}(\omega,\x)=\,f_1(\xi)f_2(\eta).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-553: Substituting this form into Eq.~\eqref{eq:schrodinger_equation_3 , we obtain the equations for $f_1(\xi)$ and $f_2(\eta)$ as follows: \begin{equation}\left[ \frac{d}{d\xi}\left(\xi\frac{d}{d\xi}\right) +\frac{\omega^2}{4c^2} \xi +\frac{\gamma_1 \, \omega}{c} \right]f_1(\xi) =0, \label{eq:f1EOM} \left[ \frac{d}{d\eta}\left(\eta\frac{d}{d\eta}\right) +\frac{\omega^2}{4c^2} \eta +\frac{\gamma_2 \, \omega}{c} \right]f_2(\eta) =0 \label{eq:f2EOM},\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\label{eq:gamma_consistency} \gamma_1+\gamma_2 = \gamma\end{equation} must be satisfied for consistency.}
elements.sent-2411-554: Let us also rewrite the boundary condition of the mode function presented in Eq.~\eqref{eq:boundary_condition , where we assume an incoming plane wave for $z<0,~|\x|\to\infty$, in terms of the new coordinates $\xi$ and $\eta$\footnote{ $|\x|\to\infty$ is equivalent to the condition $\xi+\eta\to\infty$.}}
elements.sent-2411-556: Therefore, $\eta\to\infty$ is sufficient for the original boundary condition.. \begin{equation}\varphi^{(0)}(\omega,\x)\sim e^{i\omega (\xi-\eta)/(2c)} \quad for\quad \eta\to\infty\, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-557: Consequently, $f_1(\xi)$ and $f_2(\eta)$ must satisfy the following conditions: \begin{equation}f_1(\xi) =e^{i\omega\xi/(2c)} \quad for \quad \forall\,\xi , \label{eq:f1BC} f_2(\eta) \sim e^{-i\omega\eta/(2c)} \quad for \quad \eta\to\infty . \label{eq:f2BC}\end{equation} We can see that the $\xi$ dependence of the mode function, represented by $f_1(\xi)$, is determined solely by the boundary condition.
elements.sent-2411-560: Finally, we are left with the equation for $f_2(\eta)$ \begin{equation}\left[ \frac{d}{d\eta}\left(\eta\frac{d}{d\eta}\right) +\frac{\omega^2}{4c^2} \eta +\left(\frac{i}{2} +\gamma\right)\frac{\omega}{c} \right]f_2(\eta)=0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-561: By rescaling $f_2(\eta)$ as \begin{equation}f_2(\eta) = e^{-i\omega\eta/(2c)} \tilde{f}_2(\eta),\end{equation} we obtain the alternative equation to solve for the $\eta$ dependence of the mode function: \begin{equation}\eta \tilde{f}_2''(\eta) + \left(1-\frac{i\omega}{c}\eta\right)\tilde{f}_2'(\eta) + \frac{\gamma\,\omega}{c}\tilde{f}_2(\eta) =0 .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-562: Choosing a solution that remains finite for all $\eta$, we arrive at the following analytic solution: \begin{equation}\tilde{f}_2(\eta) = e^{\pi|\gamma|/2}\,\Gamma\!\left[1-i \gamma\right] {}_1 F_1\!\left[i\gamma\,;1\,;\frac{i\omega}{c}\eta\right],\end{equation} where ${ _1 F_1\![a,b,z]$ denotes the confluent hypergeometric function of the first kind.}
elements.sent-2411-563: Finally, the analytic solution of the mode function when the mass source is located at the origin $\X=(0,0,0)$ is given by \begin{equation}\varphi^{(0)}(\omega,\x) =e^{\pi|\gamma|/2+i\omega z/c}\, \Gamma\!\left[1-i \gamma\right]\, {}_1 F_1\!\left[i\gamma\,;1\,;\frac{i\omega}{c}(r-z)\right].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-564: Here, we normalized $\varphi^{(0) (\omega,\x)$ to satisfy $\varphi^{(0) (\omega,\x)=\varphi^{(0) (-\omega,\x)^*$ and produce a plane wave with unit amplitude $e^{i\omega z/c}$ in the limit as $\gamma\to0$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-565: When $|r-z|\to\infty$, the asymptotic form of the solution is given by the following \begin{equation}\label{eq:Coulomb_wave_asymptotic} \varphi^{0}(\omega,\x) \simeq \left(1-\frac{c\gamma^2}{i\omega(r-z)}\right) e^{i\omega z/c-i\gamma \log\left[\omega(r-z)/c\right]} +\frac{\Gamma[1-i\gamma]}{\Gamma[i\gamma]}\frac{c}{i\omega(r-z)}e^{i\omega r/c+i \gamma \log\left[\omega(r-z)/c\right]}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-566: Even at sufficiently large distances from the origin, the solution is modified by a logarithmic correction to the simple plane wave propagating in the $z$ direction.
elements.sent-2411-567: The second term represents the outward spherical wave scattered by the point mass source.
elements.sent-2411-568: Now, let us consider the case of an arbitrary $\X$.
elements.sent-2411-569: By applying a translation transformation with a non-zero parameter $+\X$, the mass source position is shifted to $\X$ , and the scalar field position $\x$ is transformed to $\x+\X$: \begin{equation}\varphi^{(\X)}(\omega,\x+\X) =\varphi^{(0)}(\omega,\x) .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-570: By substituting $\x\to\x-\X$ to the above form, we obtain the following relational formula \footnote{ This relational formula does not hold if an additional system breaks the translation symmetry.}
elements.sent-2411-571: For example, in the scenario where the light is emitted from a localized light source and the boundary condition specifies outgoing flux around that source, the boundary condition would break the translation symmetry, invalidating the relational formula.
elements.sent-2411-572: Consequently, this gives the mode function solution $\varphi^{(\X) (\omega,\x)$ for the general mass source position $\X$, as explicitly provided in Eq.~\eqref{eq:mode_function_solution .}}
elements.subsection-2411-010: Mode function solution for the \schrodinger-Newton gravity model using the diffraction integral formula
elements.sent-2411-573: This section investigates the mode function solution for the \schrodinger-Newton (SN) gravity model, particularly when the mass source is not localized in the $x,y$-directions; the final expression is given in Eq.~\eqref{eq:mode_function_solution_SN_pathintegral .}
elements.sent-2411-574: The SN spacetime is given by a weak gravitational field with the following Newtonian potential, which was also given in Eq.~\eqref{eq:SN : \begin{equation}\Phi^{(SN)}(\x) =\langle\mu| \frac{-GM}{|\x-\hat \X|} |\mu\rangle_M =\int d^3 X |\mu(\X)|^2 \frac{-GM}{|\x-\X|}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-575: This serves as a single classical entity represented by the ensemble-averaged form of the QG potential over various mass source positions $\X$.
elements.sent-2411-576: Unlike the QG spacetime, which can be described by a simple central potential, the SN spacetime has reduced symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-577: Consequently, determining the exact solution of the mode function in the SN spacetime is challenging, for which the equation of motion is given in Eq.~\eqref{eq:Schrodinger_eq_SN .}
elements.sent-2411-578: We employ the diffraction integral formula presented in \cite{Schneider1999,Nakamura1999 to obtain the analytic solution of the mode function in the SN spacetime.}
elements.sent-2411-579: Our approach is based on two main assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-580: First, we adopt the eikonal approximation, which asserts that $\omega^2\varphi/c^2 \gg \partial_z^2 \varphi$.
elements.sent-2411-581: This condition indicates that the length scale over which the mode function varies is sufficiently larger than the wavelength.
elements.sent-2411-582: Second, we utilize the thin-lens approximation for gravitational lensing.
elements.sent-2411-583: This interprets that the mode function scattering occurs locally at $z\sim \langle \hat Z\rangle_\text{M}$.
elements.sent-2411-584: Next, we demonstrate the detailed derivation of the mode function solution using the diffraction integral formula.
elements.sent-2411-585: First, we rescale the mode function as follows: \begin{equation}\varphi^{(SN)}(\x)=e^{i\omega z} F(\x) .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-586: According to Eq.~\eqref{eq:Schrodinger_eq_SN , the rescaled mode function then satisfies the following equation of motion: \begin{equation}\partial_z^2 F+\frac{2i\omega}{c} \partial_z F +\left(\partial_x^2+\partial_y^2\right)F -\frac{4\omega^2}{c^2} \Phi^{(SN)}(\x) F=0 .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-587: By applying the eikonal approximation, $\omega^2 F/c^2\gg \partial_z^2 F$, we can neglect the first term of the left-hand side.
elements.sent-2411-588: This gives the approximate equation of motion as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:schrodinger_eq_SN_aprx} i\partial_z F \simeq \left[ -\frac{c}{2\omega}\left(\partial_x^2+\partial_y^2\right) +\frac{2\omega}{c} \Phi^{(SN)}(\x)\right] F .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-589: This equation resembles the time-dependent {\schrodinger equation if we interpret $z$ as time, $\omega/c$ as mass, and $2\omega\Phi^{(\text{SN ) (\x)/c$ as the potential term.}}}
elements.sent-2411-590: Now, let us obtain the solution of Eq.~\eqref{eq:schrodinger_eq_SN_aprx in the path integral formalism.}
elements.sent-2411-591: The ``Lagrangian" corresponding to this \schrodinger~equation is given by \begin{equation}L(z;\x_\perp,\dot{\x}_\perp) =\frac{\omega}{2c}\left|\dot{\x}_\perp\right|^2-\frac{2\omega}{c}\Phi^{(SN)}(\x),\end{equation} where the quantities with the subscript $\perp$ denotes the two-dimensional spatial vector in the $x,y$-direction: $\x_\perp=(x,y)$, $\dot{\x _\perp=(\dot{x ,\dot{y )$.}}}
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=0.5\linewidth]{images/path_integral.png} \caption{Path integral formalism is used to obtain the mode function solution in the SN spacetime.} \label{fig:path_integral}
elements.sent-2411-592: Using the path integral formalism, the solution is formally written as \begin{equation}\label{eq:path_integral} F(\x) =\int D\x_\perp(z) \exp\left[i\int_{z_0}^{z}dz' L(z';\x_\perp,\dot{\x}_\perp)\right].\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-594: We suppose that the initial and final slices are at $z_0=-\infty$ and $z_N=z$, respectively.

elements.section-2411-010: Detailed discussion about the which-path information indicator
elements.sent-2411-624: This section discusses the details of the which-path information (WPI) indicator $\mathcal{Q _\text{WPI (\x_A,\x_B)$, as introduced in Section~\ref{sec:WPI .}}}
elements.sent-2411-625: We will focus on two main topics: First, the proof of the logical expressions connecting the WPI indicator to gravity-induced entanglement, as presented in Eqs.~\eqref{eq:logical_E_Q and \eqref{eq:logical_E_Q_2 , and second, the demonstration that the WPI indicator is an observer-accessible quantity, as shown in Eq.~\eqref{eq:WPI_observable .}}}
elements.sent-2411-626: We begin by proving the logical expression presented in Eq.~\eqref{eq:logical_E_Q , which states \begin{equation}E(\x_A,\x_B)=0 \quad\rightarrow\quad Q_WPI(\x_A,\x_B)=0 .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-627: Since the case for the SN spacetime is trivial, we focus on the QG scenario.
elements.sent-2411-628: Recall that the linear entropy in the QG spacetime is given by Eq.~\eqref{eq:GIE_simplified , as follows: \begin{equation}E (\x_A,\x_B) =\left(\int_{-\infty}^\infty dt_+\right) \frac{\pi\lambda^2}{\hbar^2} \theta(\omega_D) \int d^3X d^3X'~|\mu(\X)|^2 |\mu(\X')|^2 \sum_{j=A,B} \left|\varphi^{(\X)}(\omega_D,\x_j)-\varphi^{(\X')}(\omega_D,\x_j)\right|^2 =0 .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-629: If the overall factor $\left(\int_{-\infty ^\infty dt_+\right)\frac{\pi\lambda^2 {\hbar^2}\theta(\omega_\text{D )$ is non-zero, then the integrand must equal zero, as the integrand comprises non-negative quantities.}}}
elements.sent-2411-631:
Next, recalling that the WPI indicator in the QG spacetime is given in Eq.~\eqref{eq:WPI_QG , we substitute the above results to obtain:  egin{equation}Q_WPI(\x_A,\x_B)
otag = rac{1}{2}\int d^3X\,d^3X' |\mu(\X)|^2 |\mu(\X')|^2 \left(\varphi^{(\X)}(\omega_D,\x_A)-\varphi^{(\X')}(\omega_D,\x_A)ight) \left(\varphi^{(\X)}(\omega_D,\x_B)^*-\varphi^{(\X')}(\omega_D,\x_B)^*ight)
otag =  rac{1}{2}\int d^3X\,d^3X' |\mu(\X)|^2 |\mu(\X')|^2 \left|\varphi^{(\X)}(\omega_D,\x_A)-\varphi^{(\X')}(\omega_D,\x_A)ight| \left|\varphi^{(\X)}(\omega_D,\x_B)-\varphi^{(\X')}(\omega_D,\x_B)ight|
otag 	imes \exp\left[i\left( \arg\left[\varphi^{(\X)}(\omega_D,\x_A)-\varphi^{(\X')}(\omega_D,\x_A)ight] -\arg\left[\varphi^{(\X)}(\omega_D,\x_B)-\varphi^{(\X')}(\omega_D,\x_B)ight] ight)ight]
otag =0 .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-632: Thus, we have successfully shown the logical expression in Eq.~\eqref{eq:logical_E_Q .}
elements.sent-2411-633: Let us also prove the logical expression given in Eq.~\eqref{eq:logical_E_Q_2 , which states: \begin{equation}E(\x_A,\x_A)=0 \quad\leftrightarrow\quad Q_WPI(\x_A,\x_B)=0 \quadfor~~\forall\,\x_B.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-634: Since the SN spacetime case is trivial, we will focus on the QG case.
elements.sent-2411-635: First, the implication from left to right can be demonstrated in a manner similar to our proof for Eq.~\eqref{eq:logical_E_Q .}
elements.sent-2411-636: Therefore, we will only present the proof of the right to left implication here.
elements.sent-2411-637: According to Eq.~\eqref{eq:WPI_QG , we have \begin{equation}Q_WPI(\x_A,\x_B)=0\notag =\frac{1}{2}\int d^3X\,d^3X' |\mu(\X)|^2 |\mu(\X')|^2 \left(\varphi^{(\X)}(\omega_D,\x_A)-\varphi^{(\X')}(\omega_D,\x_A)\right) \left(\varphi^{(\X)}(\omega_D,\x_B)^*-\varphi^{(\X')}(\omega_D,\x_B)^*\right).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-638: Note that the integrand can take either positive or negative values depending on the choices of $\X$ and $\X'$.
elements.sent-2411-639: Therefore, we cannot conclude that the integrand must necessary be zero even if $\mathcal{Q _\text{WPI (\x_\text{A ,\x_\text{B )=0$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-640: However, if $\mathcal{Q _\text{WPI (\x_\text{A ,\x_\text{B )=0$ holds for an arbitrary $\x_\text{B}$, it follows that \begin{equation}\varphi^{(\X)}(\omega_D,\x_A)-\varphi^{(\X')}(\omega_D,\x_A)=0.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-641: Substituting this result into the expression for the linear entropy given in Eq.~\eqref{eq:GIE_simplified with the detector positioned at $\x_\text{B =\x_\text{A}$ yields \begin{equation}E(\x_A,\x_A)=0 .\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-642: Thus, we can also demonstrate the logical expression stated in Eq.~\eqref{eq:logical_E_Q_2 .}
