elements.section-2411-011: Imaging process using an optical convex lens in wave optics
elements.sent-2411-657: In this section, we briefly review the imaging process using an optical convex lens, a concept well known in wave optics theory~\cite{Sharma2006,Hecht2012,Goodman2005,Kanai2013 , and commonly applied to explain image formation in telescopes or the human eye.}
elements.sent-2411-658: The imaging process discussed in the main context in Section~\ref{sec:imaging is inspired by this wave optical framework.}
elements.sent-2411-659: Specifically, we describe how an arbitrary incident wave with amplitude $\xi(x,y)$ is diffracted by an optical convex lens and how this diffraction leads to the image intensity $\xi_\text{I (x_I,y_I)$ on an image screen, as depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:optical_lens .}}
elements.sent-2411-660: Note that in this section, we do not consider any gravitational mass sources; the lens discussed here refers to an optical convex lens and is unrelated to gravitational lensing.
elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[width=0.45\linewidth]{images/optical_lens.png} \caption{Imaging process using an optical convex lens.} \label{fig:optical_lens}
elements.sent-2411-661: First, we assume that the incident wave is propagating in the $z$ direction and is entering the optical convex lens perpendicularly.
elements.sent-2411-662: We denote the amplitude of the incident wave as $\xi(\x_\perp)$, where $\x_\perp=(x,y)$ represents the transverse coordinate.
elements.sent-2411-663: After diffraction by the lens, the amplitude of the transmitted wave, $\xi_\text{T (\x_\perp)$ , is given by the following expression: \begin{equation}\label{eq:lens_Diffraction} \xi_T(\x_\perp)=e^{-i \omega |\x_\perp|^2/(2cf)} \xi(\x_\perp),\end{equation} where $f$ is the focal length of the lens.}
elements.sent-2411-664: Next, we consider that the image screen is located at a distance $f$ from the lens, and the plane of the screen is characterized by the two-dimensional coordinate $\x_I=(x_I,y_I)$.
elements.sent-2411-665: According to the Fresnel-Kirchhoff's diffraction formula~\cite{Sharma2006 , the wave amplitude on the image screen, $\xi_\text{I (\x_I)$, is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:Fresnel-Kirchhoff} \xi_I(\x_I) \propto \int_\Sigma d^2 x_\perp \, \frac{e^{-i\omega L(\x_\perp,\x_I)}/c}{L(\x_\perp,\x_I)} \, \xi_T(\x_\perp),\end{equation} where $L(\x_\perp,\x_I):=\sqrt{|\x_\perp-\x_I|^2+f^2}$ represents the distance between a point on the lens and a point on the image screen.}}
elements.sent-2411-666: The integral region $\Sigma$ denotes the finite region of the lens.
elements.sent-2411-667: Substituting Eq.~\eqref{eq:lens_Diffraction into Eq.~\eqref{eq:Fresnel-Kirchhoff and applying the far-field approximation, $f\gg |\x_\perp-\x_I|$, we obtain \begin{equation}\label{eq:imaging_formula_optics} \xi_I(\x_I) \simeq \int_\Sigma d^3 x \, e^{-i\omega \x_\perp\cdot \x_I/(c f)} \, \xi(\x).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-668: Here, the overall constant factor has been omitted.
elements.sent-2411-669: Hence, the image intensity observed through the optical convex lens can be evaluated based on the Fourier transformation of the incident wave.
elements.sent-2411-670: In Section~\ref{sec:image_formation , we discussed how to construct the Einstein ring image using observer-accessible quantities, such as the scalar field CF and the WPI indicator.}
elements.sent-2411-671: Specifically, Eq.~\eqref{eq:imaging_formula describes an imaging process that can be performed via the Fourier analysis of observer-accessible quantities.}
elements.sent-2411-672: This process is similar to that used in optical convex lens imaging, as described in Eq.~\eqref{eq:imaging_formula_optics .}
elements.sent-2411-673: Thus, the operation in Eq.~\eqref{eq:imaging_formula represents how the incident wave, corresponding to the observer-accessible quantities, would appear as an image when observed through a convex lens.}
elements.sent-2411-674: Here, note that in Eq.~\eqref{eq:imaging_formula , we use the opposite sign in the Fourier exponent compared to Eq.~\eqref{eq:imaging_formula_optics .}}
elements.sent-2411-675: The minus sign in the Fourier exponent in Eq.~\eqref{eq:imaging_formula_optics indicates that the image is inverted through the convex lens.}
elements.sent-2411-676: However, we neglect this image inversion in the main context for simplicity.

elements.sent-2411-753: This deformation indicates that the light rays propagate through the ensemble-averaged spacetime of one classical configuration, reaching the observer after escaping from the mass source positioned at $|\bm{L}\rangle_\text{M}$ and $|\bm{R}\rangle_\text{M}$.
elements.sent-2411-754: In the middle panel, where the two mass source states $|\bm{L}\rangle_\text{M}$ and $|\bm{R}\rangle_\text{M}$ are moderately separated, the image shows two overlapping arcs and a vertical fringe pattern between them.
elements.sent-2411-755: This fringe pattern represents a beat effect resulting from the composing two images with a period given in Eq.~\eqref{eq:Einstein_ring_fringe , each shifted by a distance of $\bm{O _I|_{\X_\text{L }$ and $\bm{O _I|_{\X_\text{R }$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-756: This image describes the interference between two propagations of light scattered by the mass source at $\X_\text{L}$ and $\X_\text{R}$, observable exclusively within the framework of wave optics, extending beyond the analysis of null geodesics.
elements.sent-2411-757: In the right panel, where the two mass source states are well separated, the interference fringe largely vanishes.
elements.sent-2411-758: This suggests that the light scattered from $\X_\text{L}$ and $\X_\text{R}$ approaches nearly orthogonal, reducing the interference between them.
elements.sent-2411-759: Here, a central bright spot, or Poisson spot, consistently appears in the SN model but disappears in the QG model as the mass source achieves quantum superposition.
elements.sent-2411-760: In wave optics, the Poisson spot results from the wave properties of light.
elements.sent-2411-761: The light waves arriving symmetrically from the left and right of the line of sight of the observer carry a common phase, which accumulates at the center of the screen, appearing as bright intensity.
elements.sent-2411-762: Thus, the Poisson spot appears when the background spacetime maintains parity symmetry from the perspective of the observer.
elements.sent-2411-763: For the SN spacetime, the parity symmetry is always retained in the ensemble-averaged spacetime in the current case.
elements.sent-2411-764: By contrast, the QG model represents the quantum superposition of different spacetimes, which is not parity symmetric for now.
elements.sent-2411-765: This difference accounts for the Poisson spot observed only in the SN model.

elements.sent-2411-774: Regarding the experimental realization, we also address the impact of environmental noise on our proposal.
elements.sent-2411-775: In realistic settings, even if the total system begins in a pure state, it generally evolves into a mixed state by interacting with the environment.
elements.sent-2411-776: For instance, light undergoing gravitational lensing could be disturbed by quantum interactions with the environment, such as photon-electron interactions.
elements.sent-2411-777: As a result, the Einstein ring image exhibits decoherence arising not only from QG interactions, but also from the environmental noise.
elements.sent-2411-778: In practice, such environmental decoherence is expected to be dominant, rendering the QG-induced decoherence indistinguishable.
